story.message: World mismatch: the world description states 'contemporary middle school setting' but this episode is set at 'Ridgemont High' with Jordan as 'a junior.' This needs to be resolved — either rename to a middle school (e.g., 'Ridgemont Middle') and adjust grade references, or confirm with the showrunner that Season 1 is intentionally high school and update the world bible. This is the one must-fix item.
The convergence structure (hallway -> classroom -> group project -> lunch -> reflection) is well-paced and each choice compounds logically into the next — no notes on structure itself.
Consider a one-line callback in the reflection scene acknowledging Jordan's earlier 'handle sticks' prank — small continuity texture, not required.
Alex is drawn with real specificity (the sketchbook, the two-years-and-still-no-table detail) rather than as a generic 'sad kid' — this is a good example of the world's authenticity theme.
Mia toes the line between 'mean girl' cliché and a more textured character (the unguarded 'don't tell anyone today was a good day' line in 9b) — good work resisting the flat antagonist trap.
Jordan is consistent and charming throughout without stealing focus from the player's choices — good use of a low-stakes secondary character as an emotional barometer.

The lunchroom cruelty beat (scene-6/7) is handled with restraint — Mia's mockery is 'quiet cruelty' as promised in the synopsis, not cartoonish villainy, which fits the theme of belonging without moralizing.
Good instinct not to narratively punish the 'side with Mia' path — it stays uncomfortable (7c) rather than melodramatic, which respects the audience's intelligence.

dialogue.message: Player VO like 'Great plan, Wren. Really airtight' and 'Cool. Love it here already' land as authentically teenage — dry, deflective, self-aware. No notes.

Alex's 'Apparently you need to talk to people. Rude, honestly' is a great character-voice moment — dry humor emerging from someone established as withdrawn feels earned rather than tonally inconsistent.

branchDialogue entries define four distinct branchId variants per ending scene (branch-direct-approach, branch-quiet-observer, branch-bridge-builder, branch-fitting-in-cost), but nothing in the episode schema (choices, outcomes, scenes) specifies how a branchId is selected at runtime. There is no trait-threshold, path-history, or choice-tag field mapping player behavior to a branchId. As written, the rendering pipeline has no way to know which of the four dialogue blocks to play for scene-11a/11b/11c, making this entire content block effectively orphaned/unreachable. — Suggested fix: Add a 'condition' or 'requiredChoices'/'traitThreshold' field to each branchDialogue entry (e.g., branch-direct-approach triggers when choice-1 option 'a' and choice-3 option 'a' were selected), or document the selection logic in the engine spec and reference it here. (location: branchDialogue[*].branchId)
